ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for Xiao Long Bao or chicken soup dumplings; 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03315091) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers steamed dumplings as a category; no specific code exists for chicken-filled xiao long bao. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for xiao long bao or steamed chicken dumplings as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of steamed chicken xiao long bao consists of 10 pieces, weighing approximately 200 g in total, typically served in a bamboo steamer basket.

nutrient_source.title: Xiaolongbao (steamed pork dumplings) adapted to chicken variant; reference anchored to USDA FoodData Central — Steamed Dumplings with Meat Filling (FDC ID 2103687) and HPB Singapore Foodo — Xiao Long Bao nutrient profile
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists xiao long bao (pork) at approximately 200 kcal/100 g. USDA FDC steamed meat dumplings provide corroborating macro profile. Values adapted for chicken filling: protein slightly higher (~12 g/100 g), fat slightly lower (~7 g/100 g) than pork variant, consistent with chicken-vs-pork substitution in peer-reviewed dietary composition literature. Sodium estimated from soy sauce and broth content typical of this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat-based steamed dumplings — analogous estimate based on steamed wheat dough products; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken xiao long bao. GI of 48 is estimated from steamed wheat-flour dumplings and dim sum products in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al.). Steamed wheat dough products with meat filling typically fall in the GI range 45–55 (low category). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 45.0) / 100 = 21.6, using 200 g serving with 22.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and chicken broth
hypertension.guidance: A 10-piece serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 35–42% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2400 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and avoidance of additional dipping sauces are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat flour wrapper
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 48 (low) and a glycemic load of 21.6 per 10-piece serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 5–6 pieces and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of approximately 840 mg sodium and 3.8 g saturated fat per 10-piece serving warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing chicken over pork filling reduces saturated fat compared to traditional pork xiao long bao.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Chicken Xiao Long Bao (鸡肉小笼包) is a Chinese dim sum delicacy originating from Shanghai, consisting of thin wheat-flour dough wrappers encasing a seasoned ground chicken filling and a small quantity of gelatinised chicken broth. During steaming, the broth gelatin melts to form a hot, flavourful soup inside each dumpling. The dish is served in bamboo steamer baskets, typically in portions of 10 pieces. The texture is delicate — the wrapper is soft and slightly chewy, while the filling is tender and juicy. Flavour notes include savoury chicken, ginger, sesame, and soy. Nutritionally, a 10-piece serving (approximately 200 g) provides around 396 kcal, 23.6 g protein, 14.4 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notable at approximately 840 mg per serving due to soy sauce and broth. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low category), making it a moderate glycemic load food. Clinically, individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease should exercise portion control and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces. The chicken filling makes this variant lower in saturated fat than the traditional pork version. It is a popular restaurant dish in Singapore, widely available at Shanghainese and dim sum establishments including Din Tai Fung.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for chicken xiao long bao was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a named dish. Values are adapted from steamed meat dumpling profiles in USDA FDC and HPB Foodo (pork xiao long bao), with macro adjustment for chicken filling based on established chicken-vs-pork compositional differences. GI is an analogous estimate from steamed wheat-dough dim sum products in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source profiles for steamed dumplings in USDA FDC or HPB Foodo. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the reference source profiles. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the reference source profiles. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/zero for steamed chicken dumplings with no hydrogenated fat ingredients — confirmed zero per USDA FDC steamed dumpling profile. 5. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chicken xiao long bao was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 6. Halal status is listed as false because Din Tai Fung Singapore is not certified halal; individual restaurant halal status should be verified independently. 7. Serving size of 200 g (10 pieces) is based on standard restaurant portion convention; actual weight may vary by establishment. 8. The cuisine tag 'Noodles' in the input record is not applicable to this dish and has been omitted from the output classification.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed promotional menu-style clutter from food_name; set canonical English name.
Added Chinese traditional form (雞肉小籠包) to local_names.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 for Singapore-served Chinese dish.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes per G3.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero in notes per G4 zero-macro justification rule.
Set glycemic_index to 48 (numeric > 0) with analogous estimate basis from approved GI sources.
Used only 'exact', 'closest_parent', or 'none' for all ontology match_type fields per G5.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent per Q8 rule.
Removed inapplicable 'Noodles' cuisine tag from classification.
Ensured all three health conditions use correct human_condition_name strings.
